message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>This survey study is designed to understand the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on stress among specific sub-populations of college students.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design, Settings and Participants</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>An online questionnaire was sent to the students from University of Nevada, Las Vegas between 2020 October and December to assess the psychological impact of COVID-19. A total of 2,091 respondents signed the consent form online and their responses were collected.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The Perceived Stress levels of college students were analyzed via exploratory factor analysis of a survey of college students, which was collected at the University of Nevada, Las Vegas (UNLV). An explanatory factor analysis was carried out on the Perceived Stress Scale (PSS-10) results. We subsequently analyzed each factor using stepwise linear regression that focused on various socio-demographic groups.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    A two-factor model was obtained using the explanatory factor analysis. After comparing with the past studies that investigated the factor structure of the PSS-10 scale, we identified these two factors as “Anxiety” and “Irritability”. The subsequent stepwise linear regression analysis suggested that gender and age (
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    < 0.01) are significantly associated with both factors. However, the ethnicities of students are not significantly associated with both factors.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To our knowledge, this is the first study that assessed the perceived stress of university students in the US during the COVID-19 pandemic. We showed that the PSS-10 scale could be summarized as a two-factor structure through an exploratory factor analysis. A stepwise regression approach was used and we found both of the factors are significantly associated with the gender of the participants. However, we found no significant association between both factors and ethnicity. In summary, our findings will help identify students with higher risk for stress and mental health issues in pandemics and future crises.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Strengths and limitations of this study</jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="simple">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:label>➣</jats:label>
                      <jats:p>To our knowledge, this is the first study that assessed the perceived stress of university students with PSS-10 scale in the US during the COVID-19 pandemic.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:label>➣</jats:label>
                      <jats:p>Ethnicities of the participants are not significantly associated with the perceived stress. This finding is different compared to most of the previous studies.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:label>➣</jats:label>
                      <jats:p>Only quantitative questions were used in this survey study. To gain a better understanding for the psychological impact of COVID-19 on students, qualitative questions need to be included in the future survey studies.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:label>➣</jats:label>
                      <jats:p>The fact that only the participants who completed the PSS-10 scale were included in the quantitative analysis in this study leads to potential selection bias.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:label>➣</jats:label>
                      <jats:p>The proportions of African American students and Pacific Islander students are low within the survey sample compared to the demographics of UNLV. This leads to potential volunteer bias.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: Impact of COVID-19 on College Students’ at One of the Most Diverse Campuses in the United States: A Factor Analysis of Survey Data
